section_title: INTRODUCTION TO KEY SWING POINTS AND ENGINEERING THE DAILY RANGE
content:
This teaching specifically deals with trading the key swing points. We will be revisiting the Asian Open,
London Open, New York Open, and London Close.
Engineering the Daily Range (ICT Power of 3):
- The general rule of thumb involves Asia as consolidation, London creating the high or low of the daily
range, New York being part of the expansion, and London Close creating the high or low of the day or the
opposite end of the range formed in London.
- It's important to note that this is not always the case.
- In some instances, the Asian Open will create the daily high or low. For example, the day's low can be
formed during the Asian Open, with the high forming in the London session.
- Conversely, a typical Power of 3 bullish scenario involves consolidation in Asia, then the low of the day is
created, followed by expansion throughout the rest of the day.

The London Open can create the low or high of the day, as taught with Power of 3.
- Example: The London session creates the very low of the day, lower than it was at the beginning of
trading in Asia.
London as a retracement:
- When the Asian session creates a low or high of the day and expands outside of the Asian range, a drop
down in London is typically a retracement of the initial leg or impulse leg of the intraday move.
- If bullish, we assume Asia creates the low and the market is retracing down into what would be an optimal
trade entry for a long position.
- The London Open can therefore be part of a larger move that originates from the Asian Open.

section_title: HIGH/LOW, LIQUIDITY RAID, OR CLASSIC RETRACEMENT
content:
The New York Open can also create the high or low of the daily range.
- Example: The market stays in consolidation, then drops down (assuming a bearish bias). A big news
event may come out, creating a run on liquidity above equal highs, forming a New York Open raid on
liquidity. This makes the high of the day, and the market trades lower as a result.
The New York Open can also be part of a retracement from the London Open.
- We see the low formed in London creating an impulse swing.
- Price trades back down into the New York Open, creating a nice retracement.
- It then rallies, creating the high of the day later during the New York and London Close overlap.
This is considered the classic, easiest setup to trade the New York Open:
- We must assume a bullish bias beforehand.
- A low has to be formed, showing a clear impulse swing during the London session.
- Price then retraces during the London lunch period, leading into the New York Open, where a turning
point or swing point can be traded handsomely.
- This scenario is equally effective in reverse for a bearish idea: London creates the high of the day, trades
lower during the London session, then retraces into the New York session, creating a classic continuation
on the bearish premise or down close for the daily range, expanding towards London Close. Everything
shown can be done in reverse.

section_title: RANGE EXTREMITY, LIQUIDITY GRAB, OR LONG-TERM REVERSAL
content:
The London Close can be the high or low of the day.
- Typically, if the market is bullish and London or Asia created the low of the day, London Close tends to be
the opposite end of the range.
- While it doesn't always close at the high or low of the range, it generally serves well as a rule of thumb.
In other instances, London Close can create the high of the day, even after an extended consolidation
period.
- Example: The market was in a large consolidation with equal highs. Price runs up during the London
Close, takes those highs, and creates the actual high of the day, trading lower.
- This can be done in reverse: equal lows are taken, driving down to create the low of the day.
London Close can also be a reversal point from a longer-term perspective.
- Example: The market has been trading higher during the London Close time period. It makes a reversal
on a Friday, and the following week, after opening and consolidating, begins to move significantly lower,
continuing into the following week's Tuesday. This demonstrates its potential as a reversal point.

section_title: COMBINING SWING POINTS WITH HIGHER TIMEFRAME
content:
LEVELS
To effectively use these swing points, you want to combine them with higher timeframe price levels.
- When these specific key swing points or times of day overlap with higher timeframe levels, you can
anticipate what would otherwise be expected on the higher timeframe.
- For instance, if you are watching a key resistance level on a daily timeframe, and London trades up to that
key resistance point at a specific time of day, you could be a seller at London Close.
- This would be a valid scenario even if the day was otherwise bullish, because it is hitting that higher
timeframe daily resistance price point, making it an optimal point to sell short. The same principle applies
to all key swing points or times of day.
We have explored characteristics beyond what is typically taught in ICT Power of 3, blending the rules and
providing generic characteristics for each of the four major key swing points.
Practical Exercise:
- Go through your charts and pull up a 15-minute or 30-minute timeframe.
- Look at all the times the market turns and creates significant daily highs and lows, as well as intra-weekly,
weekly, and monthly highs and lows.
- Study these turning points in reference to the higher timeframe's key support and resistance levels that
you would otherwise look for.
- When these time periods or key swing points trade to those higher timeframe levels, you will observe
significant, high-probability turning points.
